GV.OC-01.proof: Mission statement includes cybersecurity objectives. Board-approved cyber risk appetite statement aligned to strategic plan. Annual review completed Feb 2026.
GV.OC-01.notes: Reviewed quarterly by CISO and CFO.

GV.OC-02.proof: Stakeholder register maintained in GRC tool. External stakeholder expectations mapped during annual vendor review. Customer contractual requirements cataloged.
GV.OC-02.plan: Automate stakeholder notification workflows by Q3 2026.

GV.OC-03.proof: Legal/regulatory tracker in place covering GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA, SOX. Contractual obligations reviewed during procurement. Compliance team conducts quarterly gap reviews.
GV.OC-03.notes: New state privacy laws being tracked for 2026 compliance.

GV.OC-04.proof: Critical services identified but not formally documented in a BIA. IT maintains an informal list of crown jewel systems.
GV.OC-04.plan: Complete formal Business Impact Analysis by Q2 2026. Engage department heads to validate critical service dependencies.
GV.OC-04.notes: BIA template selected, kickoff scheduled for April.

GV.OC-05.proof: Some dependencies documented in architecture diagrams but no formal dependency mapping exists.
GV.OC-05.plan: Implement dependency mapping tool. Start with Tier 1 applications and map upstream/downstream dependencies by Q3 2026.
GV.OC-05.notes: This is a critical gap identified in our last audit.

GV.RM-01.proof: Risk management objectives documented in the Enterprise Risk Management Policy. Approved by board risk committee annually.

GV.RM-02.proof: Supply chain risk management strategy exists but is not consistently applied across all vendor tiers. Critical vendors assessed annually.
GV.RM-02.plan: Extend SCRM assessments to Tier 2 vendors. Integrate supply chain risk into vendor onboarding workflow.
GV.RM-02.notes: Procurement team needs training on SCRM questionnaires.

GV.RM-03.proof: Risk appetite statement approved by board. Risk tolerance thresholds defined for key risk categories. Communicated to department heads.

GV.RM-04.proof: Cybersecurity risk integrated into enterprise risk register. CISO presents to ERM committee quarterly. Risk taxonomy aligned across domains.

GV.RM-05.proof: Risk response options (accept, mitigate, transfer, avoid) documented in risk management framework. Decision criteria established.

GV.RM-06.proof: Risk tolerance informs some decisions but not consistently applied across all risk response activities.
GV.RM-06.plan: Develop risk tolerance decision trees for common scenarios.

GV.RM-07.proof: Risk management procedures documented in wiki. Annual review cycle established. Last review completed Jan 2026.

GV.RR-01.proof: RACI matrix maintained for all cybersecurity functions. Job descriptions include security responsibilities. Reviewed during annual performance cycle.

GV.RR-02.proof: Risk management roles documented in policy. CISO, risk owners, and control owners identified. Communication plan in place.

GV.RR-03.proof: CISO reports to CTO with dotted line to CEO. Board risk committee provides oversight. Accountability documented in charter.

GV.RR-04.proof: Authority delegation matrix exists. Risk owners can approve risk responses within defined thresholds.

GV.PO-01.proof: Information Security Policy suite covering 15 domains. Board-approved. Published on intranet. Annual acknowledgment required for all employees.

GV.PO-02.proof: Policy compliance monitored through quarterly audits and automated controls. Exceptions tracked in GRC tool with expiration dates.

GV.OV-01.proof: Monthly security metrics dashboard reviewed by CISO. Quarterly board reporting on program status, risk posture, and key incidents.

GV.OV-02.proof: Board risk committee meets quarterly. Cybersecurity is standing agenda item. Annual deep-dive briefing by CISO.

GV.OV-03.proof: Program effectiveness assessed through annual penetration tests and audit findings. No formal maturity assessment program.
GV.OV-03.plan: Implement annual NIST CSF maturity assessment cycle. This tracker is the first step.

GV.SC-01.proof: Supply chain risk management program exists but limited to IT vendors. Not yet extended to OT or cloud service providers comprehensively.
GV.SC-01.plan: Expand SCRM program to cover cloud and OT vendors by Q4 2026.

GV.SC-02.proof: Security requirements included in vendor contracts. Roles and responsibilities defined in standard vendor agreement template.

GV.SC-03.proof: Vendor onboarding includes security questionnaire. Oversight limited to annual reviews for critical vendors only.
GV.SC-03.plan: Implement continuous monitoring for Tier 1 vendors using security ratings service.

GV.SC-04.proof: Critical vendors assessed via SIG questionnaire. SOC 2 reports collected annually. Not all vendors assessed consistently.

GV.SC-05.proof: Standard contract clauses include breach notification, right to audit, data handling requirements, and incident response obligations.

GV.SC-06.proof: Some mitigations in place (vendor segmentation, contract requirements) but no formal supply chain risk treatment plan.
GV.SC-06.plan: Develop formal supply chain risk treatment plans for critical vendor categories.

GV.SC-07.proof: Breach notification requirements in all vendor contracts. SLA of 24-hour notification for security incidents affecting our data.

GV.SC-08.proof: No formal joint incident response procedures with suppliers. Handled ad-hoc when incidents occur.
GV.SC-08.plan: Develop joint IR playbooks with top 10 critical vendors. Schedule tabletop exercises.
GV.SC-08.notes: This gap was exposed during a vendor breach last quarter.

GV.SC-09.proof: Vendor resilience not formally evaluated. Some information gathered during onboarding but not monitored.
GV.SC-09.plan: Add resilience assessment criteria to vendor evaluation framework.

GV.SC-10.proof: SCRM is partially integrated with ERM. Supply chain risks appear in risk register but assessment methodology differs.
GV.SC-10.plan: Align SCRM assessment methodology with enterprise risk scoring framework.

ID.AM-01.proof: CMDB maintained in ServiceNow. Automated discovery scanning weekly. Hardware assets tagged and tracked. Coverage verified through quarterly audits.
ID.AM-01.notes: 99.2% asset coverage confirmed in last audit.

ID.AM-02.proof: Software inventory maintained via endpoint management tool. SaaS inventory tracked in CASB. License compliance reviewed quarterly.

ID.AM-03.proof: Network diagrams maintained for core infrastructure. Data flow diagrams exist for critical applications but not comprehensive.
ID.AM-03.plan: Complete data flow mapping for all Tier 1 and Tier 2 applications by Q3 2026.

ID.AM-04.proof: Vendor service inventory maintained in procurement system. Not fully integrated with asset management or risk systems.
ID.AM-04.plan: Integrate vendor service catalog with CMDB.

ID.AM-05.proof: Asset classification scheme implemented (Public, Internal, Confidential, Restricted). Criticality ratings assigned based on BIA results.

ID.AM-07.proof: Data inventory exists for regulated data (PII, PHI). No comprehensive data catalog covering all data types and their metadata.
ID.AM-07.plan: Deploy data discovery and classification tool. Build enterprise data catalog starting with Tier 1 systems.
ID.AM-07.notes: Budget approved for data classification tooling.

ID.AM-08.proof: IT systems lifecycle management process documented. End-of-life tracking in CMDB. Quarterly review of aging infrastructure.

ID.RA-01.proof: Vulnerability management program with weekly scanning (Qualys). Critical/High vulns tracked to remediation. MTTR metrics reported monthly.
ID.RA-01.notes: Average MTTR for critical vulns: 5 days.

ID.RA-02.proof: Subscribed to US-CERT, ISACs (FS-ISAC), and commercial threat intel feeds. Intel reviewed daily by SOC team.

ID.RA-03.proof: Annual threat assessment conducted. Threat modeling performed for new applications. Threat landscape briefings provided to leadership quarterly.

ID.RA-04.proof: Risk assessments include impact and likelihood analysis using 5x5 matrix. Documented in risk register with quantitative estimates for top risks.

ID.RA-05.proof: Risk register prioritized by inherent risk score. Risk response options evaluated and documented. Residual risk tracked.

ID.RA-06.proof: Risk responses documented but tracking of implementation is inconsistent. No formal risk response tracking dashboard.
ID.RA-06.plan: Implement risk response tracking in GRC tool with automated status updates and owner notifications.

ID.RA-07.proof: Change management process includes risk assessment. Exception process requires risk acceptance documentation with expiration.

ID.RA-08.notes: Not applicable - organization does not handle CUI.

ID.RA-09.proof: Annual enterprise risk assessment conducted. Results presented to board. Top 10 risks tracked with mitigation plans.

ID.RA-10.proof: Continuous vulnerability monitoring via Qualys and Wiz. Emerging vulnerability alerts from threat intel feeds. Automated patching for standard workloads.

ID.IM-01.proof: Post-audit improvement tracking process. Findings logged in GRC tool. Remediation plans assigned with target dates.

ID.IM-02.proof: Annual penetration test findings drive improvements. Red team exercises conducted but lessons learned not systematically captured.
ID.IM-02.plan: Formalize lessons learned process from security exercises. Create improvement backlog from test findings.

ID.IM-03.proof: Operational improvements identified informally during daily operations. No formal continuous improvement process for security operations.

ID.IM-04.proof: IR plan reviewed and updated annually. BCP/DR plans tested semi-annually. Communication plans documented and distributed.

PR.AA-01.proof: Identity lifecycle management via Okta. Automated provisioning/deprovisioning integrated with HR system. MFA enforced for all accounts.

PR.AA-02.proof: Physical access via badge system with zone-based controls. Logical access managed through IAM platform. Quarterly access reviews completed.

PR.AA-03.proof: MFA enforced for all user accounts. SSO via SAML/OIDC for cloud applications. Certificate-based auth for service accounts.
PR.AA-03.notes: Passwordless authentication pilot underway for corporate laptops.

PR.AA-04.proof: Password policy enforced: 14-char minimum, complexity requirements, 90-day rotation for privileged accounts. Breach password checking enabled.

PR.AA-05.proof: RBAC implemented. Least privilege enforced. PAM solution (CyberArk) for privileged accounts. Just-in-time access for admin operations.

PR.AA-06.proof: Network segmentation enforced. Zero trust principles applied for remote access. API gateway controls access to services.

PR.AT-01.proof: Annual security awareness training mandatory for all employees (KnowBe4). Monthly phishing simulations. Role-based training for developers and admins.
PR.AT-01.notes: Click rate on phishing sims down to 3.2% from 12% last year.

PR.AT-02.proof: Security awareness team of 2 FTEs. Training calendar published annually. Content updated quarterly based on threat landscape.

PR.DS-01.proof: Full disk encryption on all endpoints (BitLocker/FileVault). Database encryption (TDE) for sensitive data stores. Cloud storage encrypted with customer-managed keys.

PR.DS-02.proof: TLS 1.3 enforced for all external communications. VPN for remote access. IPsec for site-to-site. Certificate management automated via Let's Encrypt and internal CA.

PR.DS-10.proof: DLP policies configured in email gateway and cloud apps. Endpoint DLP deployed but coverage gaps exist for some endpoints.
PR.DS-10.plan: Extend endpoint DLP coverage to 100% of managed devices. Add DLP monitoring for collaboration tools.

PR.DS-11.proof: Backup strategy: 3-2-1 rule followed. Daily incremental, weekly full backups. Quarterly restore tests documented. Immutable backups for critical systems.

PR.PS-01.proof: Vulnerability scanning covers all assets. SBOM maintained for critical applications. Firmware updates tracked for network devices.

PR.PS-02.proof: Security testing integrated in SDLC. SAST/DAST in CI/CD pipeline. Third-party pen testing for major releases. Hardware security testing for IoT devices.

PR.PS-03.proof: CIS Benchmarks applied to all server and endpoint configurations. Configuration baselines enforced via Ansible/Chef. Drift detection automated.

PR.PS-04.proof: Change management process with CAB review. Changes tested in staging. Rollback procedures documented. Emergency change process defined.

PR.PS-05.proof: FIM deployed on critical servers. Unauthorized change alerts sent to SOC. Configuration management enforces approved states.

PR.PS-06.proof: Patch management: Critical patches within 7 days, High within 14 days. Automated patching for OS and standard software. Exception tracking in place.

PR.IR-01.proof: Security architecture documented with defense-in-depth approach. Architecture review board includes security representation. Reference architectures published.

PR.IR-02.proof: Network segmented into security zones (DMZ, internal, restricted, management). Microsegmentation for critical workloads. East-west traffic monitored.

PR.IR-03.proof: DR plans exist for Tier 1 systems. Recovery procedures tested semi-annually. RTO/RPO defined but not all systems have validated recovery capabilities.
PR.IR-03.plan: Extend DR testing to Tier 2 systems. Automate DR failover for cloud workloads.

PR.IR-04.proof: Data centers meet Tier III specifications. UPS and generator backup. Environmental monitoring (temperature, humidity, water detection).
PR.IR-04.notes: Primary cloud strategy reduces on-prem physical risk exposure.

DE.CM-01.proof: 24/7 SOC monitoring via SIEM (Splunk). NDR (Darktrace) for anomaly detection. IDS/IPS at network perimeter and between zones.
DE.CM-01.notes: Mean time to detect: 15 minutes for known threats.

DE.CM-02.proof: Physical security monitoring via CCTV at data centers. Badge access logs reviewed. Visitor management system in place.
DE.CM-02.notes: Mostly cloud-based infrastructure reduces physical monitoring needs.

DE.CM-03.proof: UEBA deployed for insider threat detection. Privileged session recording via CyberArk. DLP alerts monitored by SOC.

DE.CM-06.proof: EDR (CrowdStrike) on all endpoints. Cloud workload protection for containers. Real-time malware detection and automated response.

DE.CM-09.proof: Basic compute resource monitoring via cloud provider tools. No unified monitoring across hybrid infrastructure for security-relevant resource anomalies.
DE.CM-09.plan: Implement unified infrastructure monitoring with security-focused alerting across cloud and on-prem.

DE.AE-02.proof: SIEM correlation rules detect known attack patterns. SOC triages alerts within 15-minute SLA. Automated enrichment from threat intel.

DE.AE-03.proof: Incident analysis includes adversary objective assessment. MITRE ATT&CK framework used for classification. Impact analysis performed for confirmed incidents.

DE.AE-04.proof: Basic DLP alerting for known data exfiltration patterns. Limited ability to detect novel exfiltration techniques or encrypted channel abuse.
DE.AE-04.plan: Deploy advanced DLP with machine learning-based anomaly detection. Implement DNS tunneling detection. Add cloud API monitoring for data egress.
DE.AE-04.notes: This is a board-level concern following industry breach trends.

DE.AE-06.proof: Unauthorized access detection via SIEM rules and UEBA. Failed login monitoring with automatic lockout. Anomalous access pattern alerting.

DE.AE-07.proof: Limited detection of internal DoS conditions. Network monitoring would catch major incidents but no specific detection rules for application-layer internal DoS.
DE.AE-07.plan: Develop internal DoS detection use cases for SIEM. Add application-layer rate limiting and anomaly detection.

DE.AE-08.proof: Some lateral movement detection via EDR behavioral analysis. Limited east-west traffic analysis in flat network segments.
DE.AE-08.plan: Deploy network traffic analysis for east-west monitoring. Implement honey tokens and deception technology for lateral movement detection.
DE.AE-08.notes: Microsegmentation project will improve this significantly.

RS.MA-01.proof: Incident detection and reporting process documented. Multiple reporting channels (SOC hotline, email, ticketing). Automated alerting from security tools.

RS.MA-02.proof: Documented IR plan with playbooks for 12 common incident types. Plan tested via tabletop exercises semi-annually. Last exercise: Feb 2026.

RS.MA-03.proof: External coordination procedures documented. Relationships established with FBI, CISA, and sector ISAC. Legal counsel engaged during incidents.

RS.MA-04.proof: IR team of 4 FTEs plus on-call rotation. Retainer with external IR firm (Mandiant) for surge capacity. Annual IR training for team members.

RS.MA-05.proof: Incident severity classification scheme (P1-P4). Escalation matrix defined. Auto-escalation for P1/P2 incidents. Executive notification procedures in place.

RS.AN-03.proof: Centralized logging in SIEM. 90-day hot storage, 1-year cold storage. Log sources include endpoints, network, cloud, applications, and identity systems.

RS.AN-06.proof: Incident documentation in ticketing system. Timeline reconstruction performed. Evidence handling procedures followed. Chain of custody maintained for forensic cases.

RS.AN-07.proof: Root cause analysis performed for P1/P2 incidents. Not consistently performed for lower severity incidents.
RS.AN-07.plan: Extend root cause analysis to all P3 incidents. Implement RCA templates and training for junior analysts.

RS.AN-08.proof: Incidents classified using internal taxonomy aligned to VERIS framework. Categories tracked for trend analysis.

RS.CO-02.proof: External communication procedures in IR plan. Legal review required before external disclosure. PR team engaged for public-facing incidents.

RS.CO-03.proof: Incident information shared with ISAC on ad-hoc basis. No formal sharing protocol or automated indicator sharing.
RS.CO-03.plan: Implement STIX/TAXII for automated threat indicator sharing with sector partners.

RS.MI-01.proof: Containment procedures in IR playbooks. Automated containment actions via SOAR (isolate host, block IP, disable account). Pre-approved containment authorities defined.

RS.MI-02.proof: Eradication and recovery procedures documented. Clean rebuild processes for compromised systems. Verification steps before returning to production.

RC.RP-01.proof: Recovery plan documented as part of BCP/DR program. Recovery procedures tested semi-annually for Tier 1 systems.

RC.RP-02.proof: Recovery prioritization based on BIA criticality tiers. Tier 1 systems recovered first. RTO/RPO targets defined per tier.

RC.RP-03.proof: Backup integrity verified through automated checks. Full restore tests performed quarterly but not all systems covered.
RC.RP-03.plan: Extend restore testing to all Tier 2 systems. Implement automated restore validation.

RC.RP-04.proof: Recovery verification checklist used post-incident. Functional testing performed before returning systems to production.

RC.RP-05.proof: Business continuity procedures exist but have not been fully tested under realistic conditions. Manual failover for most systems.
RC.RP-05.plan: Conduct full-scale business continuity exercise in Q3 2026. Automate failover for critical cloud workloads.

RC.RP-06.proof: Recovery activities documented in incident tickets. Post-recovery reports generated. Lessons learned captured.

RC.CO-03.proof: Recovery status communicated to stakeholders via established channels. Executive briefings during major recovery efforts.

RC.CO-04.proof: Public communications handled reactively. No pre-approved communication templates for common recovery scenarios.
RC.CO-04.plan: Develop pre-approved communication templates for common incident/recovery scenarios. Train PR team on cyber incident communications.
